title: 5 Actors Who Basically Earned A Second Degree Just To Film ONE Scene
dek: These stars didn't fake it — they trained for months (sometimes years) to actually pull off the skill on camera, and the behind-the-scenes stories are wild.

items.heading: Natalie Portman Trained Like An Olympic Ballerina — For Over A YEAR
items.text: Natalie Portman didn't just take a few dance classes for Black Swan, she spent roughly a year building an entire dancer's body from scratch with former New York City Ballet dancer Mary Helen Bowers. By the end she was doing hours of ballet, swimming a mile, and toning daily, and she still lost her toenails and dislocated a rib along the way.

images.caption: Natalie Portman trained for roughly a year to play Nina in Black Swan

items.heading: Rami Malek Had Never Touched A Piano — Then Learned To Play It UPSIDE DOWN
items.text: Rami Malek said flatly that before Bohemian Rhapsody, he'd "never played a piano, never touched a piano," yet he still had to learn guitar and piano cues, including a scene where Freddie Mercury famously plays the keys upside down. His days on set were basically music school: hours of singing, piano, and movement training just to fake it convincingly for the camera.

images.caption: Rami Malek learned piano and guitar from zero for the role

items.heading: Margot Robbie Lived At The Rink For Five Months To Become Tonya Harding
items.text: Margot Robbie couldn't ice skate at all before I, Tonya, so she trained roughly four to five months, four hours a day, five days a week, with skating choreographer Sarah Kawahara, working straight through her own wedding week. She got good enough that Kawahara had her perform the first minute of Tonya Harding's actual 1994 Olympic routine on screen herself.

images.caption: Margot Robbie trained for months to skate as Tonya Harding

items.heading: Joaquin Phoenix Built The Joker's Creepy Dance From A 1930s Vaudeville Clip
items.text: For Joker, Joaquin Phoenix worked with choreographer Michael Arnold and then went down a YouTube rabbit hole of old performance footage, landing on 1930s dancer Ray Bolger's "Old Soft Shoe" number as the key inspiration. He said he basically stole Bolger's odd, arrogant chin-up move wholesale, and it became one of the character's most iconic physical tics.

images.caption: Joaquin Phoenix built Arthur Fleck's dancing from old vaudeville footage

items.heading: Timothée Chalamet Spent FIVE YEARS Learning To Play Bob Dylan's Harmonica
items.text: Long before A Complete Unknown even started filming, Timothée Chalamet worked with harmonica coach Rob Paparozzi for five years, eventually mastering 13 Dylan songs on guitar, piano, and harmonica at the same time. He performed every single song live on camera instead of lip-syncing, and even took a solo "spirit-gathering" road trip retracing Dylan's path from Minnesota to Greenwich Village to prep.

images.caption: Timothée Chalamet spent five years training to play Bob Dylan's music live
